title: Spaghetti carbonara
subtitle: Guanciale, eggs, pecorino. No cream.
excerpt: The Roman classic: rendered guanciale tossed with hot spaghetti, eggs, pecorino romano, and black pepper, the heat of the pasta cooking the eggs into a silky glossy sauce. No cream, no garlic. Serves two.
sourceNotes: Roman in origin, twentieth-century in its modern form. Pre-1928 public-domain Italian cookery books don't include the recipe; the canonical formula was codified post-WWII in Roman trattorie. Synthesised from the consensus method used by Antica Trattoria della Pesa in Rome and documented in Mastering the Art of Italian Cooking by Marcella Hazan (still in copyright but widely consistent with the trattoria standard).

recipe.freezeNotes: Doesn't freeze. The eggs split on reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Four is the comfortable maximum at a domestic hob; the egg-tossing step gets unwieldy past that.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Render the guanciale up to two hours ahead and hold off the heat. Everything else happens in the last 90 seconds.
recipe.temperatureNote: the only heat the eggs see is the pasta and the pan, off the burner

recipeTools.notes: For the pasta water; aim for 4 litres for 200 g of spaghetti.
Wide enough to toss the pasta in.
For the pecorino — coarse-grated cheese clumps.

glossaryTerms.definition: The Italian term for tossing pasta with sauce off the heat, using pasta water to bind the fats and proteins into a glossy emulsion. The action is rhythmic — bowl tilting, tongs lifting, water added a splash at a time.

content.text: Two hundred grams of spaghetti boiled until just al dente. A small batch of diced guanciale rendered in its own fat in a hot pan. A bowl of eggs beaten with a generous grating of pecorino and a hard twist of black pepper. The pasta lifted from the water directly into the guanciale, then the pan off the heat. The eggs poured in; the lot tossed with tongs and a splash of pasta water until every strand carries a glossy coating. Sixty seconds.

What carbonara isn't: it isn't cream, it isn't garlic, it isn't bacon, it isn't pancetta if you can get guanciale. The egg-and-cheese sauce sets gently on the residual heat; turn the hob back on at any point and the result is scrambled egg on noodles. The pan goes off the heat before the eggs go in. Always.
What you need
Method
Boil the pasta water hard

Bring 4 litres of water to a fierce boil in a large saucepan. Salt generously — a heaped tablespoon of fine salt. Drop in {{pasta-dried}} of spaghetti and stir to separate. Cook for 1 minute less than the packet's al dente time.
Render the guanciale

While the pasta cooks, scatter {{guanciale}} of diced guanciale into a cold frying pan. Set over medium heat. Cook gently for 6 to 8 minutes, stirring now and then, until the cubes have given up their fat and turned crisp gold-brown at the edges. The fat in the pan is the start of the sauce.
Beat the eggs and cheese

In a medium bowl, whisk {{eggs}} whole eggs and {{egg-yolks}} egg yolks until smooth. Stir in {{pecorino-romano}} of finely grated pecorino and {{black-pepper}} of cracked black pepper. The mixture should be a thick paste; if it's runny, add another spoon of cheese.
Lift the pasta into the pan

When the spaghetti is just under al dente, lift it directly from the water into the guanciale pan with tongs, dragging through a half-cup of pasta water on the way. Toss to coat. Cook for 30 seconds in the pan; the pasta finishes cooking, the starch in the water binds with the fat.
Off the heat, in goes the egg

Take the pan off the burner. Wait five seconds for the residual heat to drop. Pour in the egg-and-cheese paste. Toss vigorously with tongs, lifting and folding, for 30 seconds. Add a splash of hot pasta water if the sauce thickens too far. The eggs
mantecare
into a glossy sauce that clings to every strand.
Plate immediately
Twist the pasta onto two warm plates. Top each with a final grating of pecorino and another grind of pepper. Eat now; the sauce stiffens within a minute.
Troubleshooting

items.cause: Pan still on the heat when the eggs went in.
items.fix: Off the burner. Wait five seconds. Then pour and toss.
Add hot pasta water a tablespoon at a time while tossing. The sauce should pour, not lump.

items.cause: Pre-grated cheese, or grated too coarse.
items.fix: Grate fresh on a microplane. Bagged grated cheese is dusted with anti-caking starch.

items.cause: Pasta water under-salted, or guanciale skipped its fat.
items.fix: Salt the water until it tastes of the sea. Render the guanciale until the fat pools — the sauce builds on that fat.

items.symptom: Pasta cooked through but the eggs barely set
items.cause: Pan went too cold between off-heat wait and egg pour.
items.fix: Don't wait more than five seconds off the heat. Keep the pan warm in the residual hob heat if needed.

content.text: Where this dish lives

Carbonara is Roman cucina povera — five ingredients, no waste, on the table in fifteen minutes. The cured pork cheek (guanciale), the sheep cheese (pecorino), the pepper, and the egg are the four flavour pillars; the pasta is the carrier. Eat it with a glass of crisp Frascati, the way it would arrive in a small trattoria in Trastevere.
